reason: The passage (Genesis 32-33) describes Jacob's fear of his brother Esau and their subsequent reconciliation, along with Jacob's wrestling with a divine being and his journey to Shechem. While there is mention of personal tension and Jacob's apprehension about Esau's approach with 400 men, there is no description of any upcoming destruction—whether man-made, natural, or supernatural—nor any disaster intended by Yahweh affecting the general public. The narrative focuses on individual/family conflict resolution, not public-scale destruction.
